name: String landscape predictivity
summary: Flux compactifications of string theory (KKLT, Bousso-Polchinski) give an exponentially-large number of metastable vacua (~10^500 estimated for type IIB), spanning an effectively continuous range of low-energy physics. If the landscape is real and selection is anthropic + multiverse, almost any low-energy parameter combination consistent with our existence is selectable. This raises the meta-puzzle of whether string theory is meaningfully predictive in the low-energy regime, or only constrains the boundary between landscape and 'swampland' (theories not arising from any consistent UV completion).

characteristic_numbers.note: Bousso-Polchinski (2000) flux-vacuum counting with O(100) fluxes; Douglas (2003) statistical estimates. Specific number is highly uncertain but the magnitude is robust.
Landscape Lambda values are quasi-uniformly distributed; observed value is in the anthropic window for galaxy formation but special even within that window.

principles_stressed.note: If the landscape is real, naturalness becomes a statement about the prior distribution + anthropic weighting, not about Lagrangian symmetries. Swampland conjectures (Vafa 2005; Obied-Ooguri-Spodyneiko-Vafa 2018) attempt to constrain which EFTs lift to UV-complete string vacua, recovering some predictive content.

history: Bousso and Polchinski (2000), 'Quantization of four-form fluxes and dynamical neutralization of the cosmological constant', JHEP 06, 006, arXiv:hep-th/0004134 — first quantitative landscape estimate. Susskind (2003), 'The anthropic landscape of string theory', arXiv:hep-th/0302219 — coined 'landscape'. Vafa (2005), 'The string landscape and the swampland', arXiv:hep-th/0509212 — introduced swampland program. Active controversy about whether the landscape is well-defined.

references.citation: Bousso and Polchinski, 'Quantization of four-form fluxes and dynamical neutralization of the cosmological constant', JHEP 06, 006 (2000)
Susskind, 'The anthropic landscape of string theory' (2003)
Vafa, 'The string landscape and the swampland' (2005)
